title: What are Enums?
description: Create lists of unchangeable grouped constants.

slides.title: Enumerations (Enums) 🔢
slides.content:
An **enum** is a special type that represents a group of constants (unchangeable values).

To create an enum, use the `enum` keyword, followed by the name of the enum, and separate the enum items with a comma.

slides.title: Creating an Enum
slides.content: Let's create an enum grouping the levels of a game.

quizData.question: If you run the code above, what gets printed to the screen?
quizData.explanation: By default, items inside an enum are simply assigned integer values starting at 0. LOW is 0, MEDIUM is 1, and so on. Enums are just human-friendly aliases for numbers!

slides.title: Why Enums? 🛡️
slides.content:
Enums are incredibly useful for preventing bugs. If you use numbers to track a state (e.g. 0=Error, 1=Success), another programmer might accidentally assign `state = 42;`.

With an `enum`, the compiler strictly restricts values to the ones you explicitly defined in the list! You can't assign `42` to a `Level` variable.

quizData.question: Enums are a great way to restrict a variable to have one of only a few pre-defined values (like days of the week, or difficulty levels).
quizData.explanation: Yes, they make code incredibly robust and easy to read compared to raw integers.
